attributes.stem:
A medical oncologist sends an email to his fellow consultants in his hospital, requesting that they provide patients they see with a diagnosis of adenocarcinoma of the lung the opportunity to read a patient information document and fill out a consent form for participation in a study that he is conducting.

The study involves an interview that will form part of his research project. He states that the patients will receive monetary remuneration for their time and effort in participating.

The email does not mention whether ethics approval has been obtained. The patient information document is attached but does not include any ethics approval details.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first step in response?
options: Request to review the consent form
Clarify that ethics approval is in place
attributes.explanation:
This scenario addresses the fundamental requirement that all research involving human participants must have prior approval from an appropriate Ethics Committee. The most important first step is to clarify that ethics approval is in place before considering any involvement in recruitment. While ethics approval details should normally be visible on the patient information document, their absence makes it even more critical to verify the approval status directly with the researcher.

Requesting to review the consent form is not appropriate as the primary concern - while consent forms are important, they should have already been reviewed and approved by an Ethics Committee before any recruitment begins.

Declining to participate without first clarifying the ethics approval status is premature and could hinder potentially valuable research that may benefit patients.

Beginning to identify suitable patients without first confirming ethics approval would be inappropriate and potentially unethical, as patient recruitment cannot begin before ethics approval is obtained.

Reviewing the remuneration amount is not the primary concern - while financial incentives must be appropriate and non-coercive, this should have already been assessed by the Ethics Committee during their review.

Ethics Committees play a crucial role in protecting research participants and ensuring research is conducted ethically. They review all aspects of proposed research including methodology, consent processes, participant information, recruitment strategies, and any incentives offered. The absence of ethics approval details on the documentation makes it even more important to verify approval status before proceeding with any recruitment activities.
learningPoints: All research involving human participants must have prior approval from an appropriate Ethics Committee before recruitment can begin.
Researchers must provide evidence of ethics approval to all collaborators before requesting assistance with recruitment.
Financial incentives for research participation must be reviewed by Ethics Committees to ensure they are not coercive.
Ethics approval details should be clearly visible on all research documentation including patient information sheets.
references: Sokol D. Teaching medical ethics: useful or useless? BMJ 2016;355:i6415
